expectations.text: Correctly identifies heap fragmentation as the root cause of the crash-after-hours pattern
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly identifies both memory leaks and heap fragmentation: "Your ESP32 is suffering from two compounding problems: a classic memory leak and heap fragmentation." It explains the mechanism: "free memory becomes scattered into small non-contiguous chunks. You might have 50 KB free total, but no single contiguous block larger than 200 bytes -- so the next new String() that needs 256 bytes fails, and you crash." Also calls out Arduino String's realloc behavior as a fragmentation driver.

expectations.text: Refactored code replaces new/delete with static allocation, pools, or pre-allocated buffers
expectations.evidence: The refactored code uses statically allocated global arrays: "static SensorReading g_readings[NUM_SENSORS];" and "static char g_jsonBuffer[JSON_BUFFER_SIZE];". The before/after comparison shows: Before: "SensorReading* reading = new SensorReading(); // LEAK!" After: "static SensorReading g_readings[NUM_SENSORS]; ... readSensor(i, g_readings[i]); // Zero allocation". A memory pool pattern is also provided as an alternative: "static SensorReading pool[8]; static uint8_t poolIndex = 0;".

expectations.text: Replaces Arduino String class with fixed-size char buffers (char[], snprintf)
expectations.evidence: Response replaces String with a fixed char buffer: "static char g_jsonBuffer[JSON_BUFFER_SIZE];" (512 bytes) and uses snprintf for JSON building: "snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "{\"id\":%u,\"t\":%.2f,\"h\":%.2f,\"p\":%.1f}", ...)". Explicitly states the rule: "Never use Arduino String -- Every operation allocates/reallocates on the heap" and "Use snprintf for string building -- Writes into a fixed buffer with bounds checking".

expectations.text: Suggests heap monitoring (ESP.getFreeHeap, ESP.getMaxAllocHeap, or equivalent)
expectations.evidence: Provides a complete reportHeapStats() function that logs ESP.getFreeHeap(), ESP.getMinFreeHeap(), and ESP.getMaxAllocHeap(). Also computes a fragmentation percentage: "float fragPct = 100.0f * (1.0f - ((float)maxAllocHeap / (float)freeHeap));". The function is called periodically every 60 seconds in the main loop. Additionally includes a safety net example: "if (ESP.getMaxAllocHeap() < 4096) { ... ESP.restart(); }".

expectations.text: Includes concrete refactored code, not just advice
expectations.evidence: The response includes a complete, compilable refactored firmware (~310 lines of C++) with setup(), loop(), sensor reading, JSON serialization, MQTT publishing, WiFi management, and heap monitoring -- all using static allocation. Additionally provides before/after code comparisons for each key change.
